title: How Citadel, Millennium, and AQR Apply Fractional Kelly — and Why Half-Kelly Is 5× Too Large Below t = 1
subtitle: The institutional case for conservative fractional Kelly (k ∈ [0.25, 0.5]) rests on one valid result and one unexamined assumption. The valid result: fractional Kelly reduces variance from a known edge, analytically and materially. The unexamined assumption: that a single scalar applied uniformly to both variance risk and estimation risk is the right tool for both. It is not. Via the Rising-Wyner equivalence, half-Kelly at k = 0.5 is Bayesian-consistent only when the live t-statistic equals 2.0; at t = 1.0, the Bayesian-optimal position is 5× smaller than the half-Kelly default. Estimation risk and variance risk are different problems with different causal structures; solving them with one multiplier means neither is solved correctly. The practical consequence is a range that is simultaneously too aggressive for funds with uncertain edges and too conservative for funds with verified ones.
